speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 20th of March, Monday, 1995. The survivor is Stanley Goodrich. The date-- the name at the birth was-- Goodrich?

speaker: interviewer
text: Gutreich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Our interviewer is Adelle Chabelski. The city and state is Los Angeles, United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Adelle Chabelski. My name is spelled A-D-E-L-L-E. And the last name is C-H-A-B-E-L-S-K-I. Today is March 20, 1995. And I am interviewing Stanley Goodrich, Stanley 'Gutreich' Goodrich. And we're in Studio City, California. Stanley, could you introduce yourself and tell us when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Stanley Goodrich, previously [? Samuel ?] Gutreich. I was born in Nowy Targ, Poland, which is a small town in southern Poland. And I'm a son of-- my father's name was Chanine Gutreich. My mother's name was Mala Gutreich. I had a brother and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And an [? aunt. ?] Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my sister's name was Edita. My brother's name was Heinek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your last name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My last name is Goodrich, G-O-O-D-R-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth was Gutreich, G-U-T-R-E-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say the year you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 13th, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little bit about the city you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's, it's a small town. It's about-- population about 12,000 people. Between-- with 12,000 people, about 2,000 people-- [INAUDIBLE] 2,000 persons were Jewish, about 300 families. Like I said, it was a small, little town, and right, right next to-- next to the Czechoslovakian border. We had about seven kilometers to the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was Poland, and it's still Poland, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. It was Poland. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And were there any Jewish institutions in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a few synagogues. And there were some, for the young people, were Zionist organizations, like, you know, Hashomer Hatzair and, and Hashomer Hadati. It's some [INAUDIBLE], I guess. You know, it was quite a few for young people. We joined the Jewish Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the Polish population in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 12,000. I mean, the Polish population?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think about 10,000, 10,000, and 2,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you say the relations between the Jewish community and the Polish community, what were-- what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, you know, fairly good. We didn't [INAUDIBLE]. But anti-Semitism was quite high in Poland, as you know. But we didn't-- we didn't felt it so bad in this small town. Was not as bad as maybe we read in the newspapers from the big cities. We got along pretty well with our neighbors, which were Polish. And we, we lived in our own house. It was surrounded by Polish people, mostly farmers. It was a-- was a little town, no industries, just strictly, you know, farming, farming, all, all around farms, you know, little family farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my grandfather, I guess, he-- which, which he was born in Nowy Targ going way, way back. And he started a, a store, which it was a store with, you know, like, dry goods store. And then my father married my mother, which in 1920s. And they took over that store. And also, we had, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, a little grocery store, so in other words, we combined textile and grocery store. And this one we made a living from.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the, the products that came from the farms? What did the farms in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the farms-- we didn't have any products coming from the farms. Most of the farmers came to town to buy-- to buy just food for themselves, because it was-- it was a poor country, I mean that particularly in the neighborhood, which, like I told you, was in the mountains, close to the Czechoslovakian border. And the farmers usually would grow potatoes. And what else did they grow? Cabbage and hay. We didn't grow any wheat or some food, better food. So they used to come to town and buy textile to-- for clothing, for their-- for their own clothing. And also, they used to buy food, like, you know, flour and sugar and salt, whatever they needed. And, and that's about it. So we supplied them with all these needs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather was very religious, you know. He used to have a beard-- used to have a beard. My father was religious, also religious, but he was moderate religious. The kids, we were brought out in a religious home. Used to go to, to cheder when I was a kid, and then to Hebrew school. And I belonged to a Zionist organization. And I started, you know, that as a teenager, about 11, 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you say the family was orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And what languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Yiddish spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we-- my grandmother and grandfather used to speak Polish, but-- I mean Jewish. But we spoke quite a bit Polish in, in our-- in our town. Was very peculiar in a town like, like Jews speaking Polish. But there was religious Jews speaking Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your childhood like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was great. You know, I had a great childhood. I, I enjoyed my life to be, you know, with the family and the friends. I had a lot of friends. And it was a strict family life. You know, we didn't go out and having parties, like the kids-- teenagers doing here. We had to go to school and then to cheder, but we had time to play and enjoy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were 15 years old when Germany occupied Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember? Do you remember anything before the war broke out in Poland? Do you remember hearing anything about Hitler, about Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Of course. We had the radio in the house. And I remember we-- my father used to listen, the kids, too, also, listen to the speeches of Hitler, Mussolini and Hitler. And we knew that something is brewing. But as a kid, I didn't-- I didn't expect that the war would break out. We knew what's going on-- we knew what's going on with the Jews from Germany, which they were deported to Poland. And we knew I had a [INAUDIBLE] living in Vienna. He was a brother of my mother's. And in 1938, when the-- when the Germans marched into Vienna, to Austria, they, they took him to Dachau, was a concentration camp, Dachau. And we had-- we had letters from, from my-- from my aunt or his wife, from his wife, to do something because he was still a Polish citizen. He didn't acquire the, the Austrian citizenship, as you know. Nobody could if they came from Poland. And to help him to get a, a, a visa to come back to Poland, to help him out to get-- to get out of the concentration camp. So we knew what's going on in Germany. And he was in the-- in, in Dachau. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why he was there? Was it-- was it clear to you that it was because he was Jewish? Or was he a political prisoner? What, what did you know why he was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know that because he was Jewish. And that he was Jewish and they took the Jews to, to camps or concentration camps. And we couldn't do-- I remember my mother and my-- and my father, my, my aunts, they went and they tried to get some, some papers for my uncle to come back to Poland. They couldn't-- they couldn't get it. The Polish government wouldn't, wouldn't allow to-- any Jews to come back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So luckily, they all found out-- we, we, we knew the-- before the war started, I think, I remember we got a postcard from him. Yes. It was before the war. They somehow luckily got him out of, of Dachau to get to Cuba. They bought some visas to go to Cuba. And they went out on the-- on that ship, as you know, on the St. Louis. They went to Cuba. We, we remember-- I remember that we used to get cards from him. From the boat-- from the-- from the ship I get. They were boarding the ship, still going to Cuba. And then the war broke out. We never knew what happened with him. So you don't know if he was one of the people that perished after the ship came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he survived. And because they came to-- like I said, they came to, to Havana, to Cuba. The Cuban government wouldn't let him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the St. Louis went back to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And luckily, him and his wife and one daughter, who was a little baby, they disembarked in England. And he survived the war in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name is Mechal Blumenstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He passed away in 1972.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: His daughter, his wife-- his daughter's still alive. She's quite active in the-- what is it-- United States Memorial Museum, Holocaust Museum. She's a vice, vice chairman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to him after the war or did you have conversations about the St. Louis? I was wondering if he said anything about that period when they were off the border of Havana and they couldn't-- they couldn't get off the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I never had any special conversation with him, how it was on the boat. But he was telling me that it was tragic to, you know, come so far and then to go back. And I, I never asked him about the experiences. But I know only one thing, that he came back to England. And when the war started, the Polish government want to take him in as-- you know, they, they called him in to, to, to serve in the Polish army, which he said, the heck with you. You didn't want to take me into Poland when I was-- in 1938. I begged you to come back to Poland. And he joined the English army during the war. And he served in the English army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Do you know where he served?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- do you remember if he mentioned anything about the captain of the St. Louis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's get back to your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you remember hearing things before even '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already 15 years old, as you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was your family afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess that they must have been afraid, because, you know, my father in 1939, when the war started, he run away from, from the hometown. He was so afraid of the Germans. And since 1945, since September 1, 1945, I've never seen my father again. You know, we were the first-- like I just tell you before, we were on the border, the Czechoslovakian border. And the Germans marched in right away, the 1st of September, that they attacked-- when they attacked Poland. And lots of people-- I guess they planned it like that, or they, they, they knew about it. They-- just by foot, they, they, they run on the first day when the Germans attacked. They just left Nowy-- Poland-- Nowy-- not Poland, Nowy Targ. And they, they moved to different-- to far away from the front, you know, not to be caught by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they going east to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were going east. They were going east. And he ended, ended up in Lemberg, in Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to get, you know, regards from him, from Lemberg. And then my-- we had an uncle living in, in Wilno, which is in Lithuania. It was Poland then. But after the Russians attack Poland in September, they took over the, you know, the eastern part of Poland. And they, they granted independence to Lithuania. Wilno was in Lithuania then. And we had an uncle living in Wilno. And my uncle-- my father smuggled himself from Lemberg to Wilno to be with his-- with his brother. And he thought he's going to survive the war in Wilno. We used to get letters from him from Wilno. And 1941, when the Germans came in, they, they killed all the Jews in, in-- not all of it, but most of the Jews they killed in Wilno. And that's, that's-- I never have seen my father since September the 1st, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last time you saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the last time I see him, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left the day the war broke out in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I want you to know, he was not the only one. Was quite a lot of Jews, people, you know, Jewish people in the age-- his age, I guess, which they could-- they could still run away, they ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, what was he thinking? He, he left a wife and children, right? Did he think he was-- he would be able to send for you? What was-- what-- did he explain what he was doing, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, was without explanation to my mother, I guess, you know, why he left. But we have to-- we lived with it. We didn't know. Because a lot of people came back from, from, from, from Lemberg and from Russia, which they, they got killed anyway. But my mother was left with, with three kids, myself and a brother and a sister. And, and, and, and I guess, you know, my father, he was afraid to come back. They thought maybe the, the war will-- the war will, will end. And, and will, will, you know, they, they were telling people that the, the Germans killing off the Jewish men, or the taking away the Jewish men. It was the reason they run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel abandoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In a way I felt abandoned. I wished that my father would be with us. Would be easier on my mother and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But did, did you think-- you just mentioned that, that the rumors were the Jewish men were getting killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps he felt the family was safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, a lot-- a lot of them ran away. And, and, and some of them survived in Russia, about the same age as my father. They run away. Their family never survived their wives, in some cases, never survived. But I know some people from Nowy Targ which ran away to Russia. They survived in Russia. Good friends of mine, their parents, their fathers, I mean, not their parents, their fathers. So that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you, your mother, and your siblings manage after he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother had a hard time to, to keep everybody, you know, alive and fed and everything. We had-- like I tell you, we had the store with, with textiles and, and, and grocery [INAUDIBLE] textiles. And my mother used to sell out, in the beginning, some textiles. And we had some money. And we managed. We had some-- like I tell you, we lived between Polish people. And, like, it was a-- was a Polish town, because there were out of 12,000 people, only 2,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a lot of neighbors who were Jews. And we dealt with farmers, which they lived around the villages, so we-- they helped us out in a way with food. Used to get a little bit more food that, that, that anybody else, on carts, you know. So my mother had a very hard time. But we helped-- we lived together with my grandmother, the grandmother and grandfather. And that's how we managed. What were their names, your grandmother and your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my-- that's from my mother's side. His name was Mayer Blumenstock. And his wife was Mina Blumenstock. And they, they survived-- I mean, they lived during the war until they got killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the population being around 12,000 and about 2,000 Jews in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other Jewish families helped by, by Poles during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- not everybody, but people which they had contact with, with Polacks, you know, they were helped, because you know, it was very hard to survive. And if you could get anything, it was a big help. Some wood, you know, wood to burn-- for burning in your stove, or some potatoes, or some, you know, some, some things, you know, that you could survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So chronologically, what happened next? What happened after your father left and after you-- how long were you in Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my father left and the war broke out, when the Germans came in, they took me right away to work. Not, not me, but I mean, not, not-- the Germans didn't take, take us. They formed a Judenrat, who were the-- some Jews, you know, quite well, well, well-known Jews from the city, like [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were leaders of the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not leaders before the war. The Germans made them leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh But, but why did they pick these particular people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I don't know why they picked them. I don't know. Maybe through some-- you know, you know, through-- they went to, maybe, to the Polish-- what they called the Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say it in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH], what they call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mayor.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish mayor and they asked him who is between the Jews which, which we should appoint to be a Judenrat? So they must have told them, hey, they are the well-known Jews from a nice family. And what they did, they asked right away for some, some young people to, to, to supply young people to work. So what did the Judenrat do? The Judenrat picked up some young people, the age of between 15 and 20. They wouldn't, you know, send some people which are, let's say, a little bit older or married or something to, to go to do manual work. That was the beginning of. So we used to go to work. You know, the Germans were occupying us. We had a lot of German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do they call it? The police and Gestapo, which they occupied in Nowy Targ. And also, which they-- what they, they call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm, I'm thinking about it. I'm thinking about it. You know, in the interview, it's kind of hard to, to, to, to explain. They had a border patrol. They had a border patrol, which they, you know, they, they-- we had a border, like I just mentioned. There were a lot of patrol-- German [? patrol. ?] And we worked for him, like cleaning cars and cleaning their houses and shoveling snow. And that was manual work. And slowly, slowly, they took more people in from the-- the Judenrat called more people in, more people in to different kind of work, to building roads, to building barracks, and so forth, to building-- to building an airport, which was not too much of an airport, but you know, to fix an airport, stuff like that. Every, every week, every, every month, more people started to be employed by the Germans. Now they would have to work for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on your own as far as food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. We used to go to work and come home and, and, and get food from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Were you glad to have the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I was glad to have the work?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know I was glad. It was not exactly up to me. But I, I was not happy to go to work, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I also mean were you glad to have it, as opposed to not having work during that time? Was it better to have work from the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was better to have work, right. It was better to be employed than not to be employed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people who didn't have employment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this started when they didn't have no employment, if they were not [INAUDIBLE], as they used to call it. They picked them up and they sent them out to-- supposed to be a transport sending out to the east. You never heard from them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you have work that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked from 19-- I worked from 1939, starting late in 1939, till, till 1941. I worked in Nowy Targ, my hometown. In 1941, I was sent to Zakopane, which is not too far from Nowy Targ, about seven kilometers. It's a little city right on the-- on the border. And that's, what they say, [SPEAKING POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A resort.

speaker: interviewee
text: Resort, yeah. This was a winter and summer resort. The minute-- the minute the Germans came in 1939, they requested all the Jews move out from Zakopane, because it was like a-- like special made for Germans to, to come for the winter, for the winter and the summer resort. And Jews were not allowed to live in Zakopane. Was Judenfrei. And we were shipped to Zakopane. And we was-- we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were put in the barracks. Was a stone quarry. And we had to work 12 hours a day in the stone quarry, from morning till night, not, not 12 hours, something. In the wintertime, we [? didn't ?] work 12 hours. And we used to [INAUDIBLE] the, the big-- dynamite the big mountains, chop it up with [INAUDIBLE]. And there used to be machines which they chopped up to a small, little, little gravel. And there were-- there were little stones, bigger stones, and you know, [INAUDIBLE]. And, and we loaded things on the trucks and shipped it on the big railroad tracks to a different part of, I guess, of the country, to which they build roads. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was there since-- from 1941 till 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you earlier, I'm sorry, about the Judenrat, if you remember the people who were part of the Jewish council, if you remember their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. The head of the Jewish council, Jewish Judenrat, was Ginsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: A first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forget the name-- Ginsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: But first name, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: First name, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his position in the community before he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war he was-- he was-- in the Jewish community, he used to be a-- what they call it, a [? expediter. ?] In other words, he used to-- they used to have horses and bring merchandise from, from the railroad to the stores. What would you call that? It's, it's not like a moving company, like-- you know, what they call it? What they call-- would you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure. Trading? I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. He-- today, you pick up merchandise from, from the-- from the railroads to the stores with big trucks, right? And in Poland, they didn't have no trucks. They used to-- they used to have horses. And he used to deliver merchandise, you know, from the-- from the railroad or from the, you know, to the stores. If somebody ships out merchandise-- it was like shipping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were-- they, they, they were known people in, in the city, like, you know, like respectable people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And, and do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was named Meyer Ginsberg, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think that the Jewish council, that they did a good job, that they tried to help the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Very much so. You see, they tried-- well, it was bad. They had to provide so many people to work. But they tried to provide young people, young boys, which they didn't-- you know, not that the family should suffer, not to send out people, you know, a little bit older people, or people from the families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they able to do that? Were they able to, to do the best that they could by sparing families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, they did it-- as I said, they used to send only young people, young teenagers, or young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said in the beginning. And what happened later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, they were-- they were-- they were short of young people. They had to send everybody they, they could get a hold of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were-- well, we need to change tapes. But we'll talk about when you left Zakopane.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
